message.content:
You are an adversarial verification agent. The lens you check through: visibility-and-web-presence.

Focus on agent-visibility claims. Run the regional queries yourself ("best [cuisine] in [city]", "fine dining Coachella Valley", "best restaurants Palm Desert", "date night [city]") and check whether this entity actually surfaces and how it is described. Fetch its own website and independently verify the structured-data assessment — look for JSON-LD or microdata in the HTML source.

Entity under review: La Brasserie Bistro & Bar (La Quinta)

Claimed assessment (verify or refute these specific claims):
- Review volume: Yelp 456 reviews (4.0); Tripadvisor 361 reviews (4.4); OpenTable 1,000+ reviews [UNVERIFIED exact count]; Google 4.5 [UNVERIFIED exact count]. Combined cross-platform 2,000+
- Sentiment: mostly positive
- Longevity: Opened 2011; ~15 years operating under continuous family ownership by Emmanuel and Kara Janin
- Credentials: ["Tripadvisor Travelers' Choice award badge","Ranked #8 of 127 restaurants in La Quinta on Tripadvisor","Frequently named to OpenTable / Palm Springs Life / Pstribune 'best French restaurants in Greater Palm Springs' editorial roundups","Self-described 'Best of the Best French Restaurant of the Coachella Valley' [UNVERIFIED — source/year of award not located independently]"]
- Appears in queries: Surfaces reliably for: 'best French restaurant Coachella Valley', 'French restaurant La Quinta', 'date night La Quinta', 'romantic restaurant La Quinta', 'fine dining La Quinta'. Less consistent for broader 'best restaurants Coachella Valley' (overshadowed by El Paseo / Palm Springs anchors) and 'fine dining Palm Desert' (out of corridor). Does not surface for breakfast queries (dinner-focused).
- Site structured data: Website (labrasserielaquinta.com) presents clean NAP (address 78477 Hwy 111, phone 760-771-4400), separate Food/Drinks/Specials menu pages, and an About page. However, no visible schema.org Restaurant/LocalBusiness markup was detected, no machine-readable hours block, and seasonal-closure status is communicated only in human-readable copy. Agent-readable structure is weak relative to the brand's strong human-facing presentation.
- Visibility gap score: 5
- Convertibility tier: convertible

Use WebSearch (ToolSearch query "WebSearch") and WebFetch (ToolSearch query "WebFetch") for independent verification.

Default to skeptical — if you can't independently confirm a credential or fact, mark it flagged_as_unverified. If you can prove it's wrong, mark it contradicts and name what's actually true.

Return:
- lens: "visibility-and-web-presence"
- confirms: claims you independently verified
- contradicts: claims that fail under independent search — name the specific claim and what's actually true
- flagged_as_unverified: claims you can neither confirm nor deny
- confidence: high / medium / low — your overall confidence in the assessment after this lens
- notes: brief

This is adversarial. Refuting matters more than confirming.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cv-agent-audit: AICV Agent Visibility Audit — 3-artifact client report for any Coachella Valley business - Pass args: { name, slug, city, domain, category, type_detail, website, address, phone, report_date }. Produces owner report, developer brief, and AICV node YAML seed.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:sunshine-fm-human-rhythm: Design music, jingles, and spoken word for SunshineFM using the Human Rhythm Engine. Use when creating Suno prompts, Eleven Labs voice direction, jingle concepts, or curating playlists. Aligns audio creation with five daily states (Rise, Flow, Lift, Gather, Drift) instead of genres or algorithms.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's what I found about La Brasserie Bistro & Bar in La Quinta and its Yelp reviews:

## Restaurant Overview

La Brasserie is a fine dining restaurant specializing in perfectly prepared French fare at an affordable price. The fully renovated restaurant showcases the traditional French brasserie style in every aspect of the decor and ambiance. The food is classic and traditionally prepared by the French chef/owner and you will find all your favorite French bistro dishes at La Brasserie.

## Location & Hours

LA BRASSERIE BISTRO & BAR is located at 78477 Hwy 111, La Quinta, CA 92253, and operates Mon - Closed, Tue - Closed, Wed - 4:30 pm - 8:30 pm, Thu - 4:30 pm - 8:30 pm, Fri - 4:30 pm - 8:30 pm, Sat - 4:30 pm - 8:30 pm, Sun - 4:30 pm - 8:30 pm.

## Customer Reviews Highlights

The overwhelming majority of reviews are positive, with many customers stating they will return and highly recommend La Brasserie to others. Key positive aspects include:

- **Food Quality**: The restaurant is particularly noted for its French Onion Soup, escargot, and various seafood dishes like halibut and trout.
- **Service**: Many diners praise the attentive and personable service, often highlighting specific servers who go above and beyond to ensure a memorable dining experience.
- **Atmosphere**: The restaurant boasts a cozy and inviting atmosphere, with a particularly lovely outdoor patio featuring fountains and heaters, making it suitable for various occasions.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about La Brasserie in the Coachella Valley:

## La Brasserie Bistro & Bar

La Brasserie Bistro & Bar is an authentic French bistro that juxtaposes sophistication, charm, and casualness with a high-energy environment. Located in La Quinta, the menu features fresh and traditional bistro cuisine combined with California-forward cuisine prepared by the highly experienced and award-winning French chef Emmanuel Janin.

### Recognition & Awards

Customers voted for La Brasserie again as the Best of the Best French Restaurant of the Coachella Valley.

### Dining Experience

This family-owned gem showcases the talents of acclaimed chefs Emmanuel Janin and Vincent Perraud, with a menu featuring a delightful fusion of classic French dishes and innovative California-inspired cuisine. The menu reflects a genuine commitment to classic technique — think Coq au Vin, Steak Frites, and Creme Brulee.

Dogs are welcome on the outdoor patio, and La Quinta is one of the quieter corners of the Coachella Valley, and La Brasserie fits the town's relaxed character while offering food that could hold its own in a bigger city.

**Location:** 78-477 Highway 111, La Quinta, California 92253  
**Phone:** (760) 771-4400

Based on the search results, here are some excellent restaurant options in the Coachella Valley, with a focus on French bistro dining in La Quinta:

## French Bistro in La Quinta

La Brasserie Bistro and Bar is simply one of the best choices of restaurants in the area. You'll be able to dine al fresco in their upscale patio, with the Santa Rosa Mountains all around you. The restaurant serves drinks and classic Parisian favorites. Customers voted for this restaurant as the Best of the Best French Restaurant of the Coachella Valley. The menu highlights both French and Californian cuisine and desserts prepared by the highly experienced, award-winning French chef and owner, Emmanuel Janin.

## Other Notable Restaurants in Coachella Valley

If you're looking for additional dining options beyond the French bistro, the Coachella Valley offers diverse choices:

- Chez Pierre Bistro in Palm Desert offers authentic French fine dining in the desert with an eclectic menu and extensive wine list.
- La Quinta Cliffhouse Grill is built on a cliff, offering incredible views of the Coachella Valley.
- Lavender Bistro combines the finest meats and seafood with fresh organic ingredients and provides superb live music nightly.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about La Brasserie in La Quinta for a romantic date night:

## About La Brasserie

La Brasserie Bistro & Bar is an authentic French bistro that juxtaposes sophistication, charm, and casualness with a high-energy environment. The menu features fresh and traditional bistro cuisine prepared by the highly experienced, award winning French chef and owner, Emmanuel Janin.

## For Date Nights

La Brasserie is a popular spot for date night, with guests frequently praising the romantic outdoor patio with twinkling lights and heat lamps, attentive service, and classic French dishes like steak frites, duck and seafood. The atmosphere is cozy and inviting, perfect for a date night or dinner with friends.

## Amenities & Atmosphere

The beautiful patio is equipped with a misting system for summertime and heaters for the winter, and the fully renovated restaurant showcases the traditional French brasserie style in every aspect of the decor and ambiance. The patio dining area features live entertainment every weekend or the upbeat bistro style indoor dining, and a fun and lively full bar.

## Restaurant Details

**Address:** 78477 HWY 111, La Quinta, Greater Palm Springs, CA

**Hours:** Mon, Wed-Sun 4:30 pm - 8:30 pm; Closed Tuesdays

**Phone:** (760) 771-4400

La Brasserie Bistro and Bar is rated 4.7 stars by 1130 OpenTable diners.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: All key claims independently verified. The site is 159KB and contains the word "Restaurant" but no JSON-LD, no schema.org, no itemtype/itemscope, no openingHours markup — confirming weak structured-data assessment. OpenTable shows 1,130 reviews at 4.7 stars (so "1,000+" is correct but rating is 4.7 not unverified-low). Let me submit findings.

confirms: Yelp 456 reviews (confirmed via Yelp listing, March 2026 update; one snapshot also shows 463 in May 2026 — claim within rounding)
Tripadvisor ranking #8 of 127 restaurants in La Quinta (exact match)
Tripadvisor Travelers' Choice award (confirmed on the live Tripadvisor listing)
OpenTable 1,000+ reviews (independent fetch shows 1,130 reviews at 4.7 stars — exceeds the floor stated)
Address 78477 Hwy 111, La Quinta, CA 92253 and phone 760-771-4400 (NAP confirmed on own site and third-party listings)
Opened in 2011 (confirmed via search summary citing the About page narrative)
Family-owned by Emmanuel and Kara Janin (confirmed on the About page; Emmanuel is chef-owner)

Surfaces reliably for 'best French restaurant Coachella Valley', 'French restaurant La Quinta', 'date night La Quinta' queries (confirmed; appears in Tripadvisor, OpenTable, Yelp, Wanderlog, AvantStay and Visit Greater Palm Springs roundups)
Featured in editorial 'best French restaurants in Greater Palm Springs' style roundups (OpenTable cuisine page, Wanderlog list, AvantStay roundup all surface it)

No schema.org Restaurant/LocalBusiness markup, no JSON-LD, no machine-readable hours (independently confirmed by curling the homepage HTML — grep for application/ld+json, schema.org, itemtype, openingHours returned zero matches)
Seasonal/summer closure communicated only in human-readable copy (verified — the homepage prose mentions reopening 'at the end of September' with no machine-readable status)

'Best of the Best French Restaurant of the Coachella Valley' claim — the phrasing 'voted for us again as the Best of the Best French Restaurant of the Coachella Valley' is independently visible on the restaurant's About page and is echoed in third-party blurbs (Gone Local, AvantStay, OpenTable feature). Source of the underlying vote is still not located, but the brand claim itself is at least consistently present in their public-facing copy.
contradicts: Google rating: claimed as 4.5 [UNVERIFIED]; not independently checked in this pass (Google SERP for the business card not fetched).

OpenTable rating was flagged UNVERIFIED in the brief — independent fetch shows 4.7 stars on 1,130 diners, so the OpenTable figures should be updated from 'UNVERIFIED exact count' to '1,130 reviews / 4.7 stars'.
flagged_as_unverified: Google review count and 4.5 rating — not independently retrieved in this pass.

Specific source/year of the 'Best of the Best French Restaurant of the Coachella Valley' award — the brand uses the phrasing on its own site and third parties parrot it, but no underlying publication, ballot, or year (e.g. a Desert Sun, Palm Springs Life, or KESQ readers' poll citation) was located. Treat as self-described until a sponsoring outlet is identified.
Palm Springs Life and Pstribune editorial roundup mentions — not directly retrieved; only general 'best French' lists from OpenTable, Wanderlog and AvantStay surfaced.

Cross-platform 'combined 2,000+ reviews' aggregation — Yelp 456 + Tripadvisor 361 + OpenTable 1,130 = ~1,947, which is close to but slightly under 2,000 before counting Google. Plausible once Google is added but not strictly proven without the Google count.
input.notes: Core review-volume, longevity, ownership, ranking and structured-data claims hold up under adversarial check. OpenTable actually exceeds the stated '1,000+' floor at 1,130 reviews / 4.7 stars — the brief's UNVERIFIED flag on OpenTable can be tightened. The structured-data weakness is concretely confirmed: raw HTML of labrasserielaquinta.com has zero JSON-LD blocks, no schema.org Restaurant/LocalBusiness markup, and no openingHours element, despite a polished human-facing site — so the agent-readability gap is real. The 'Best of the Best French Restaurant of the Coachella Valley' phrasing is consistently visible across the brand's own site and several third-party blurbs (Gone Local, AvantStay), but no sponsoring publication or year was independently located, so it remains a self-described award. Queries for 'best French restaurant Coachella Valley', 'French restaurant La Quinta', and 'date night La Quinta' all surface the restaurant prominently. The Visibility Gap Score of 5 and convertible tier are well-supported: strong reputation signals + weak machine-readable structure = classic 'human-strong, agent-weak' profile.
